result:
I keep thinking about the word “enough.” It’s small, four letters stitched together like a piece of fabric you could hold up to the light and still see through. Yet it weighs more than most boulders. I’ve watched it bend grown adults into apology shapes, watched it chase ambitious teenagers through bright hallways until they duck into windowless rooms and call it caution. Somewhere along the way I started collecting moments when I felt the word land on me, soft as a cat but with claws that hooked under the skin. I keep them in a tin box under the bed: the afternoon I got a 97 on a chemistry test and my mother asked what happened to the other three points; the party where I told a joke that made everyone laugh and then spent the ride home replaying whether I’d hogged the air too long; the first poem I placed in a national magazine, my name finally italicized and respectable, and how I still scrolled past strangers’ congratulations feeling like they must have confused me with someone worthier. Each memory is a ticket stub I can’t quite throw away, proof I was briefly present in the stadium where “enough” was handing out lanyards that read “Almost.”

The problem, I think, is that we treat enough like a destination, a train whose arrival we keep announcing over the intercom of our lives. If I just publish one more essay, lose ten more pounds, fold the laundry inside-out so the seams don’t show—then the doors will hiss open and we’ll step onto the platform of final satisfaction. But down the track there’s always another station being built. The map keeps unfolding like one of those enchanted scrolls in fairy tales, adding mountain ranges and fjords nobody mentioned at departure. Meanwhile the coffee gets cold, the dog needs walking, and friends invite us to impromptu gatherings we half enjoy, half endure, checking our phones for news from the city of Enough, which never quite sends the confirmation code.

I read once that the universe is expanding at 74 kilometers per second per megaparsec. I don’t entirely know what a megaparsec is, but it sounds like a place where mathematics admits how staggered it feels by distance. The stars grow farther apart every moment, carrying their planets with them like children clutching lunchboxes in a divorce. If existence itself refuses containment, why do we expect our résumés to fit inside a shoebox? Why do we speak of life balance as though it’s a ledger sheet that actually tallies to zero instead of an improvised dance routine performed on the deck of a ship that’s already slipping over the horizon? My neighbor spends his weekends pressure-washing the driveway like he can blast the creeping mildew of contingency off the concrete. I wave, then go inside and scrub imaginary stains from my own projections. Both of us believe, in some unspoken way, that cleanliness is next to immortality.

There’s a thrift store downtown where I sometimes wander among the discarded evidence of other people’s former lives: cracked porcelain wedding cake toppers, coffee mugs that say “World’s Okayest Dad,” a velvet painting of a jaguar whose eyes have lost their original paint so it looks constantly startled. The price tags imply that nothing keeps its value, not even our most tender declarations. Still, every object once sat in someone’s cart under fluorescent grocery lights, chosen with the secret hope that ownership might equal allegiance. I buy a ceramic rooster for two dollars and set him on the kitchen windowsill where dawn strikes his chipped tailfeathers. He reminds me that absurdity is the only honest religion, cock-a-doodling at the sun like the answer to every question might be “Yes, and also no.”

When my grandmother began to forget, she collected hotel pens and wrote herself notes that spiraled into paper chains of anxiety: “Remember the heater, remember the cat’s thyroid pill, remember you are lucky.” The last phrase always broke my heart most. Lucky was a word she wielded like a talisman against the encroaching dark, a small candle insisting on its own flame even when she no longer recognized my name. After she died I found a note stuffed in the toe of her favorite slipper: “The day is a glass boat; do not drop it.” I pictured tomorrow as translucent and buoyant, something we carry together like children passing a bubble across a playground, terrified and thrilled by its shimmer. She taught me that memory is just another word for love refusing to leave on schedule.

I think about time not as a line but as one of those layered gelatin desserts you slice with a spoon: each flavor bleeding slightly into the next, cherry meeting lime in a pinkish gradient that shouldn’t taste good yet somehow does. My twenties feel like a strata of fluorescent Jell-O shots, all adrenaline and food dye. My thirties are more pomegranate, tart seeds you keep crunching between molars while pretending you understand tannins. What I’m saying is that chronological order is a polite fiction we agree on so history textbooks can close neatly. Inside the bloodstream, decades mingle like guests who’ve noticed the host is drunk and rules are optional. I keep meeting versions of myself in dreams—age nine with a missing front tooth, twenty-three in a nightclub bathroom telling a stranger she is worthy, forty-eight stirring soup while rain marches across the skylight—and we pass advice like contraband: “Don’t wait,” “Breathe deeper,” “The scar on your knee is a fossil of every time you ever got back up.”

Some mornings I wake convinced I owe the world a grand performance, a dazzling proof I deserve oxygen. I pace the room rehearsing accomplishments the way other people practice apologies. But then the neighbor’s kid rides by on a bicycle the color of highlighter ink, laughing at something invisible, and the moment cracks open like a geode to reveal that being alive is a permission slip we keep forgetting we already signed. I stand barefoot in the grass, earth soft from last night’s rain, and let the tiny blades write green sonnets against my ankles. The mailbox holds nothing but coupons and a postcard from someone else’s vacation, yet sunlight pools on the kitchen table like warm honey insisting on sweetness.

I once believed that if I wrote the perfect sentence it would stitch the unraveled sleeve of the cosmos. These days I suspect perfection is just fear wearing a tuxedo. Imperfection is the braver outfit: mismatched socks, spinach between teeth, laughter that snorts at its own punchline. I’ve started collecting failures like baseball cards: struck out on a karaoke high note; mispronounced “epistemological” in front of a roomful of grad students; sent a text meant for my best friend to the plumber (“I’m spiraling, bring chips”). Each cringe-worthy moment is proof I keep showing up to the stadium, glove laced, ready for whatever weird pitch the universe hums across the plate. The scoreboard never tells the whole story; sometimes the crowd erupts for a sacrifice bunt that moves a runner who later scores on someone else’s double, glory distributed like potluck dishes.

I want to tell you that the secret is to love the unfinished draft, the half-planted garden, the voicemail you forgot to send. I want to climb to the rooftop and announce that enough is a language we invented because we were terrified of open endings. But I’m standing here barefoot, word count ticking toward the thousand-mark like a gentle countdown, and I still don’t know whether I’ve filled the suitcase or merely overpacked. Maybe the answer is to zip it shut on the lip of possibility, let the train depart without us for once, and trust that motion is motion even when you can’t pronounce the next station. The coffee’s reheated, the dog noses her leash, the ceramic rooster keeps watch over the day’s slow turning. Somewhere a megaparsec stretches its legs and the stars lean farther into spaciousness. I pocket the crumpled note—“The day is a glass boat”—and step outside, arms full of ordinary mercies, already enough and still becoming.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
